response:
### Structured Table: 3 Pros & 3 Cons of Remote Work
| Category | Core Benefit/Challenge                          | Detailed Supporting Rationale                                                                 |
|----------|-------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Pro      | Customizable Schedule & Workspace Autonomy      | Employees tailor hours to caregiving, neurodivergent needs, or peak productivity (e.g., 6am–2pm shifts) and build ergonomic/sensory-friendly setups (standing desks, noise-canceling tools) unavailable in standard offices. |
| Pro      | Dual Cost Savings (Employers & Employees)       | Employees cut commuting (gas, transit), work attire, and office meal costs; employers reduce overhead for physical office space, utilities, on-site amenities, and commuter benefits. |
| Pro      | Geographically Unrestricted Talent/Job Access   | Employers hire global top talent without relocation costs; employees apply to roles across cities/countries (e.g., a rural educator qualifying for a remote edtech role in a major metro) without moving. |
| Con      | Pervasive Work-Life Boundary Blur               | No physical "office exit" ritual hinders disengagement; remote workers face unspoken pressure to respond to after-hours messages (e.g., 8pm Slack pings) or extend shifts for cross-time-zone teams, increasing burnout risk. |
| Con      | Collaboration & Communication Friction          | Spontaneous "watercooler" brainstorming (linked to 25% of workplace innovations, per Gartner) is lost; async communication (Slack, email) risks tone misinterpretation, and new hires lack in-person onboarding context to navigate team dynamics. |
| Con      | Professional Isolation & Career Stagnation Risk | SHRM data shows remote workers are 35% less likely to be considered for promotions due to "out of sight, out of mind" bias; informal mentorship (e.g., quick senior colleague desk-side feedback) and on-site networking are severely limited. |

*Table counts: 3 distinct pros, 3 distinct cons, with evidence-based rationales and concrete examples.*
